title: How I Built Undetectable Browser Automation with CSPRNG
description: A deep dive into the cryptographic random number generator and human-matched distributions that make the Crown Bot invisible to anti-bot systems.
author: Kevin

content.body: When I started building the Crown Bot for sweepstakes casino automation, the first problem wasn't the casino logic — it was detection. Anti-bot systems analyze behavioral patterns, and Math.random() creates predictable timing that gets you banned within hours.

content.heading: The Math.random() Problem
content.body: JavaScript's Math.random() uses xorshift128+, a deterministic PRNG. From just a few outputs, you can reconstruct the entire sequence. Anti-bot systems exploit this to detect automation with near certainty.

callout.text: If your automation uses Math.random() for delays, you're already detected. The distribution is uniform and the sequence is predictable.

content.heading: Enter CSPRNG
content.body: crypto.randomBytes() taps the OS entropy pool — CryptGenRandom on Windows, /dev/urandom on Linux. Output is truly unpredictable and impossible to reconstruct.

content.heading: Human-Matched Distributions
content.body: CSPRNG alone isn't enough — you need distributions that match real human behavior. I use three distributions mixed by probability:
list: **70% Triangular** (peaked at 30% of range) — fast clicks and quick typing
**25% Gaussian** (bell curve) — normal navigation and reading
**5% Exponential** (long tail) — occasional long thinking pauses

content.heading: The Results
content.body: After implementing CSPRNG with human-matched distributions, the Crown Bot has been running 22 accounts for 3 months with zero detection. The visual test page shows the keyboard and mouse behavior in real-time.

callout.text: The combination of CSPRNG + human distributions + bezier mouse movement makes automation statistically indistinguishable from human behavior.
